title: Victoria sponge with buttercream
subtitle: The classic two-layer sponge, finished with buttercream instead of cream
excerpt: A Victoria sandwich filled with raspberry jam and a simple vanilla buttercream in place of whipped cream. The buttercream holds at room temperature and makes the cake easier to transport and serve.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the Victoria sandwich recipe. The standard has been a 225 g / 4-egg sponge since Victorian baking guides. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the creaming method for butter sponges. Buttercream as a filling for layer cakes appears in British household guides from the early 20th century as a more stable alternative to fresh cream.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted sponge layers wrapped individually. Defrost at room temperature before filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge keeps in an airtight tin for 2 days. Assemble on the day of serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beating fat and sugar together until pale and significantly increased in volume before adding eggs and flour. The process incorporates air into the fat, which expands in the oven and lifts the cake. Under-creamed batter produces a denser cake; a properly creamed mixture looks pale, almost white, and feels light.

content.text: The Victoria sandwich is an equal-weight recipe: equal weights of butter, sugar, flour, and egg. The 225 g version for a 20 cm tin and four eggs is the British standard. Buttercream here replaces cream, which means the assembled cake can sit out for several hours without weeping or collapsing.

attrs.prepNote: a good one, seedless if preferred

content.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease two 20 cm sandwich tins and line the bases with baking paper. Use the

: beat the butter and sugar for 5 minutes until very pale and light. Beat in the eggs one at a time, adding a spoonful of flour with each egg after the second to prevent curdling. Fold in the remaining flour and baking powder. Stir in the vanilla and enough milk to bring the batter to a slow dropping consistency. Divide evenly between the tins and smooth the tops.

Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the sponge springs back when pressed lightly in the centre and a skewer comes out clean. Do not open the oven door before 20 minutes. Leave in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a rack and leave to cool completely before filling. Filling a warm sponge melts the buttercream.

Beat the butter until soft and pale. Beat in the icing sugar in two additions, then the vanilla and milk. The buttercream should be smooth and spreadable but hold a peak. Place one cooled sponge on a cake stand or plate. Spread the jam over the surface, then spread the buttercream over the jam. Set the second sponge on top, gently pressing down to level. Dust with icing sugar to finish.
